Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Roger Bacon

c. 1219 – c. 1292

Perception, signs, and experimental psychology in a Franciscan key.

Roger Bacon does not leave us a single treatise called “philosophy of mind.” What he does leave is something more dispersed and, in some respects, richer: a theory of soul and intellect in his early works and Communia naturalium; a detailed philosophy of perception in the Perspectiva; a causal theory of species in De multiplicatione specierum; a theory of signs, language, mental concepts, and interpretation in De signis; an account of experimental and inner experience in the Opus maius; and a moral-epistemic program in which cognition, virtue, language, theology, and natural science are inseparable. Recent scholarship even describes the mature project as a “new metaphysics” integrating language study, natural science, metaphysics, and morals, with the Opus maius as a preliminary text for that enterprise.

The reconstruction that follows treats Bacon’s references to God and angels not as marginal religious decoration but as structural parts of his account of mind. For Bacon, the human intellect cannot be understood apart from the divine Agent Intellect, from illumination, from the “uncreated truth” in which truth is finally learned, and from angelic intellects as finite spiritual agents capable of secondary illumination and causal operation. That does not make his theory anti-empirical. On the contrary, Bacon’s distinctive position is that mind is embodied, experiential, optical, semiotic, morally conditioned, and illuminated. The result is not modern physicalism, not Cartesian dualism, and not a merely theological anthropology. It is a hierarchical, causal-semiotic, illuminationist philosophy of mind.

1. The central thesis

Bacon’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as follows: the human mind is the intellective power of a spiritual soul united to a body; in this life it knows the world through sense, internal sense, species, signs, memory, cogitation, and experience; it is personally individuated against Averroist theories of a single shared intellect; it is directed toward singular things rather than toward universals as isolated abstractions; it requires divine illumination for truth, certainty, first principles, and moral wisdom; and it exists in a hierarchy that includes animal sensitive cognition below it, angelic intellects above it, and the divine intellect or Agent Intellect as the principal source of intellectual light. Bacon’s Perspectiva and De multiplicatione specierum give the causal-perceptual infrastructure, De signis gives the semiotic-intentional infrastructure, and the Opus maius gives the experiential, theological, and moral completion of the theory.

In contemporary terms, Bacon’s mind theory is strongly externalist: cognition is not primarily an inner theater of private ideas. The object of knowledge is the thing known, while species are the causal-representational mediators by which things act on sense and intellect. His semantic theory likewise tends toward direct reference: words immediately signify things rather than mental representations, although mental concepts themselves are signs or representations of things.

It is also deeply participatory. The mind does not merely observe a neutral external world. The world acts upon the mind through species; the mind receives, orders, judges, remembers, and signifies; God illuminates; angels can secondarily cleanse, illumine, and arouse; moral virtue clarifies cognition; vice and ignorance obscure it. Bacon’s theory of mind is therefore at once natural-philosophical, epistemological, semiotic, theological, and ethical.

2. Terminological map: anima, mens, intellectus, species, intentio, signum

The broadest term is anima, soul. It includes vegetative powers, sensitive powers, and intellective powers. “Mind,” in the narrower sense, corresponds chiefly to mens, intellectus, ratio, and the rational or intellectual soul. But Bacon’s theory of mind cannot be restricted to intellect alone, because human intellectual knowledge depends upon sensation, imagination, memory, cogitation, language, signs, bodily condition, and experiment.

Several Baconian terms are especially important.

Species does not mean “biological species.” In Bacon’s causal theory, species is a likeness, power, image, force, or first effect through which an agent acts beyond itself into a medium and recipient. Modern readers should not reduce it either to a mental image or to a purely physical ray. It is a causal-representational vehicle. The Stanford Encyclopedia emphasizes that in De multiplicatione specierum Bacon offers a universal theory of physical action, not merely a theory of logical species or classification.

Virtus is power or force. It is often closely associated with species, and Bacon’s explanations of natural action often rely on the propagation or multiplication of such powers. An older but useful summary of Bacon’s Opus maius says that his fundamental natural-philosophical maxims are matter and force, with the latter called virtus, species, imago agentis, and related names; natural change is explained as the impression or transmission of such power.

Intentio can mean intention in the ordinary sense, but also mental content, signifying intention, or an object as grasped by a cognitive power. This is crucial for Bacon’s account of signs and language.

Signum, sign, is anything that signifies to a sense or intellect. Bacon’s theory of signs is not merely linguistic. It includes natural signs, conventional signs, animal signs, human signs, mental concepts, and signs offered to the intellect. In De signis, the sign is relational: something becomes a sign for someone capable of conceiving through it.

3. The soul as embodied spiritual substance

In Bacon’s early works, the soul is a spiritual substance united to the body. He accepts a hierarchy of powers: vegetative, sensitive, and intellective. At this early stage, he holds that the soul has both a potential intellect and an agent intellect. The potential intellect is directly connected with sensitive powers and is oriented toward the singular material thing, while the agent intellect is directed upward and knows spiritual beings. Bacon also allows a kind of innate confused knowledge, not as Platonic ideas already possessed in full clarity, but as a disposition inclining the soul toward truth and first principles.

This early view is already important for a philosophy of mind because it refuses a simple opposition between sensory embodiment and intellectual cognition. The possible or potential intellect is not a sealed-off rational chamber. It is linked to sensitive powers and to singular material objects. Even in the early period, Bacon’s mind is world-directed, body-mediated, and singular-oriented.

In the later works, especially around the Opus maius and Communia naturalium, Bacon’s position changes decisively on the agent intellect. He comes to identify the Agent Intellect with God, the source and agent of illumination, and he attacks the view that agent and potential intellect are parts of the soul. His soul treatise in Communia naturalium treats topics such as the production of soul-parts, the sensitive soul, the unity and plurality of the intellectual soul, the composition of the rational soul, the powers of the soul, the vegetative soul, and the parts of the intellectual soul.

Bacon also distinguishes the origin of different soul-powers. The intellectual soul is created by God, while vegetative and sensitive powers are produced through nature. This matters because human mentality is not simply generated from matter in the way plant or animal life is; yet human mentality is also not detached from natural processes. The human being is a hybrid site: a created intellectual soul united with naturally generated organic powers.

There is a real interpretive tension here. Some recent work, especially by Yael Raizman-Kedar, emphasizes that Bacon naturalizes the intellect more than older scholarship admitted: corporeal species may enter the intellect through the senses without a separate act of abstraction from matter, and Bacon’s rational soul itself is treated within a matter-form framework. But Jeremiah Hackett cautions against turning this into a full naturalistic reading: for Bacon, material species are necessary for knowing things in the world, but not sufficient; divine illumination remains central, and Bacon is still a medieval substance dualist in an important sense.

The best formulation is therefore this: Bacon is not a Cartesian dualist, because he does not isolate mind from body, perception, species, or nature. But he is also not a reductive naturalist, because the intellectual soul, divine illumination, spiritual substances, and moral purification are indispensable to his psychology.

4. Singulars, universals, and the anti-Averroist individuality of mind

Bacon gives unusual importance to singular individuals. Modern interpreters disagree about how to classify his metaphysics: some see proto-nominalist tendencies, others see extreme realism, and others emphasize direct intellectual perception of the singular. What is clear is that Bacon does not make the universal, considered apart from the individual thing, the primary object of knowledge.

This is crucial for philosophy of mind. Bacon’s mind is not primarily a universal-form extractor. It is first a cognitive power directed toward singular things. The singular material thing matters for sense, for intellect, for naming, and for experimental knowledge. Even when universals are formed, they arise in a cognitive economy grounded in encounter with individuals.

This also supports Bacon’s rejection of Averroist monopsychism, the view that there is one possible intellect shared by all humans. Bacon distinguishes the object known from the representations or intelligible species through which it is known; the same object can be represented in different intellects, and knowledge is multiplied with individual souls.

So Bacon is an individualist about human intellect. There is no single human mind behind all human thinkers. Each human being has an individuated intellectual soul, and knowledge exists in a plurality of knowing subjects. This has consequences for education, communication, moral formation, and error: minds must be individually illuminated, trained, corrected, and purified.

5. Species as the causal infrastructure of mind

The doctrine of species is the backbone of Bacon’s philosophy of mind. Bacon’s De multiplicatione specierum develops a general theory of action: every efficient cause acts through a force or species by which it produces effects beyond itself. This includes corporeal and spiritual substances, sense, intellect, and matter. In the Opus maius, Bacon says that every efficient cause acts through its force, which it exercises on the matter before it; the species produced by substance and accident, by spiritual and corporeal realities, acts on sense, intellect, and matter.

This is not merely a theory of vision. It is a universal causal model. Light is the privileged example because it is visible, geometrically tractable, and central to optics, but light is not the whole theory. The Spanish-language commentary and translation work by Naranjo explains species as an alteration or impression introduced by an agent in a receiver, and as the effect of every natural agent upon receivers including sense and intellect. An Italian thesis on Bacon’s scientia experimentalis similarly stresses that Bacon turns species into the technical vehicle of causality in De multiplicatione specierum: light becomes only a particularly evident case of a more general multiplication of species, and optics becomes central because it studies this multiplication according to geometrical principles.

For mind, the consequences are large.

First, perception is not a bare contact between a passive soul and a ready-made object. It is a causal process in which the object acts through species, the medium transmits, the organ receives, and the soul’s powers judge.

Second, cognition is not detached from natural causation. Knowing belongs to the same universe in which fire heats, light illuminates, magnets attract, and celestial bodies influence. Mind is not outside nature; it is a superior kind of recipient and agent within a hierarchy of natural and spiritual causality.

Third, representation is not arbitrary. A species is a likeness or causal similitude of the agent. Thus mental content is grounded in a real relation to the object, not merely in convention or private association.

Fourth, the species theory helps Bacon integrate body and mind without collapsing one into the other. The species can be material or spiritual, corporeal or intellectual, natural or divine in origin. Bacon’s system tries to explain how a corporeal world can be cognitively present to a spiritual soul without reducing the soul to matter.

6. Perception: vision, the active eye, and the inner senses

Bacon’s Perspectiva is not merely an optical manual. It is a philosophy of perception and mind. It studies the physics of light, the propagation of species, the anatomy and function of the eye, the optic nerve, the common nerve, and the role of inner cognitive powers such as the cogitative sense. The Stanford account notes that Bacon follows an intromission theory of vision, in which visual species come from the object to the eye, while also retaining an active role for the eye.

This active role is philosophically important. Bacon’s perceiver is not a wax tablet. The eye and soul are not merely struck by external impressions. The organ is structured, active, and ordered; the inner senses coordinate and judge; the cogitative power discriminates and organizes. Bacon’s philosophy of mind is therefore neither passive empiricism nor rationalism detached from sense.

The visual process can be schematized as follows: the external object multiplies its species through the medium; the eye receives the species; the visual apparatus transmits and organizes; the common sense and cogitative power integrate and judge; memory preserves; intellect can then know, compare, name, and reason. This is not a modern neurological theory, but it is a sophisticated layered model of perception.

The Perspectiva also reveals why mathematics matters for mind. For Bacon, the propagation of species through lines, angles, reflection, and refraction means that visual cognition is geometrically conditioned. A philosophy of mind that ignores optics will misunderstand perception. This is one reason Bacon sees mathematics as indispensable for natural philosophy and even for theological understanding.

7. Internal senses, cogitation, memory, and animal minds

Bacon’s psychology is especially interesting because he attributes significant cognitive complexity to nonhuman animals. He does not say animals reason in the full human sense, but he does grant them powers of discrimination, memory, communication, and quasi-inferential organization. In Anselm Oelze’s analysis, Bacon says animals cannot properly reason, yet they can gather many things into one by a natural industry or instinct in a way resembling premises and conclusion. Bacon also ascribes a cogitative power to animals, allowing them to proceed in an orderly way toward particular ends.

This makes Bacon’s theory more graded than a simple “humans think, animals merely sense” model. Animals have a sensitive soul capable of structured cognition. They distinguish individuals, remember, communicate, and act means-to-end. The cogitative power gives them something like sensory-level inference, though not formal syllogistic reasoning or reflective awareness of premises and conclusions.

Memory is also graded. Bacon distinguishes imperfect memory associated with sense, common to animals, from more complete memory involving retention after an interval and recall in the absence of the object, which is characteristic of humans. Oelze’s account emphasizes that animal memory is often tied to recognition of a present object, whereas human memory can more fully recall absent things.

There is also a debated question about whether Bacon grants animals some access to universals. Oelze notes that Bacon can describe a dog as distinguishing a man from wood or a dog from a human, and some interpreters ask whether this amounts to cognition of a universal. The safer reconstruction is that animals can classify or discriminate in a quasi-universal way through sensitive cogitation, but they do not possess the human intellectual grasp of universals as such.

Bacon’s sign theory reinforces this richer animal psychology. He recognizes vocal sounds produced by animals and persons as signs originating in the sensitive soul with communicative intent. Non-rational animals can therefore communicate by signifying vocal sounds, even though fully conventional linguistic imposition belongs to rational, deliberating intellect.

The human mind, then, is continuous with animal sensitive cognition but not reducible to it. Human beings share perception, memory, affective signs, and cogitative organization with animals; they surpass animals through intellect, rational language, experimental science, moral deliberation, full memory, and illumination.

8. The human intellect: possible intellect, agent intellect, and illumination

Bacon’s mature view of the intellect is one of the most important parts of the reconstruction. In the Opus maius, the possible intellect is described as the capacity by which the soul is able to receive sciences and virtues from another source. The active intellect flows into minds and illuminates them for knowledge and virtue; it is not a part of the soul but a separate intellectual substance. Bacon invokes Alfarabi, Avicenna, Aristotle, and Latin theological authorities to support this separation of the active intellect from the human soul.

He explicitly argues that if the active intellect were part of the soul, the soul would be both knowing and ignorant in a problematic way. He also insists that the description “always in act” belongs properly to God, not to the human rational soul or to angels. Human souls and angels are not identical with the divine active intellect.

This gives Bacon a distinctive theory of intellectual agency. The human possible intellect is a real receptive and cognitive power. It is not erased. But the principal source of intellectual actuality and illumination is God as Agent Intellect. The human mind is therefore neither autonomous in a modern secular sense nor passive in a merely occasionalist sense. It receives species from the world, works through sense and cogitation, reasons, learns, experiments, and is illuminated by God.

Bacon’s model can be described as a threefold cooperation: the world supplies species; the human soul receives, orders, and seeks; God illuminates and actualizes truth. The mind is genuinely active, but its highest cognitive success depends upon a higher light.

9. The mind of God as Agent Intellect and uncreated truth

The divine mind is not a peripheral doctrine in Bacon. It is central to his mature account of intellect. Bacon identifies the Agent Intellect with God, the source and agent of illumination. He interprets the Aristotelian active principle not as a part of the human soul but as an external active source present in the soul by its force or influence rather than by its substance.

Bacon’s primary text draws on Augustine: the rational mind is subject to God alone in its illuminations and in its principal influences; although angels may cleanse, illumine, and arouse human minds, God has the principal influence, like the sun whose light enters through a window. Bacon also reports the Augustinian claim that we do not learn any truth except in the uncreated truth and eternal laws.

This should be treated as a philosophy of mind claim, not simply as piety. It says that truth is not produced solely by human psychological operations. The human mind knows truth by participating in a higher intellectual light. God is not just the creator of minds; God is the principal noetic source by which minds reach truth.

The Stanford article on divine illumination helps place this in context: divine illumination is the doctrine that cognition requires special divine assistance, and in the thirteenth-century Franciscan context it was often invoked to explain certainty, first principles, and knowledge that requires an unchangeable divine mind. Bacon’s version is especially ambitious because he combines illumination with sensory species, optics, experimentation, and signs.

The divine mind performs several philosophical functions in Bacon’s system.

First, God is the source of intellectual light. The Agent Intellect actualizes the human possible intellect.

Second, God is the ground of truth. Truth is not merely correspondence established by human judgment; it is ultimately rooted in uncreated truth and eternal laws.

Third, God is the first source of cognitive and moral order. The human mind is not ordered only toward technical knowledge but toward wisdom, virtue, salvation, and happiness.

Fourth, God is causally supreme within the species framework. Bacon says God produces forces when he creates bodies out of nothing, while created substances act through their own powers or species.

Fifth, divine illumination is linked to experience. Bacon does not oppose illumination to experience. In his account of experimental science, external sense experience is necessary but insufficient; inner experience involves the intellect being aided by divine illumination.

This is where non-English scholarship is helpful. A French study by Hoffmans warns against reading Bacon’s empiricism as merely material or sensory. It argues that Bacon’s appeal to experience can look like English empiricism, but his empiricism also “plunges” into intelligible and divine reality through a double experience: external experience through the senses and a higher internal experience. That reading is old, but it captures something essential: Bacon’s “experience” is not Humean sense-impression empiricism. It is sensory, experimental, intellectual, moral, and illuminative.

10. Angels and angelic minds

Bacon’s angelology also belongs inside, not outside, his philosophy of mind. Angels are finite intellectual substances. They are not the Agent Intellect, because the Agent Intellect as always actual is God. Bacon distinguishes angels, intellects, and human souls partly by their relation to bodily union: the human rational soul can be united to a body; angels cannot be united to bodies in that way.

Angels nevertheless can affect human minds. Bacon says that angels may cleanse, illumine, and arouse our minds in many ways, but God remains the principal influence. His Augustinian comparison is philosophically elegant: angels are like someone opening a window, while God is like the sun whose light enters.

This makes angels secondary noetic agents. They do not replace divine illumination, and they do not become the principal source of truth. They mediate, prepare, assist, arouse, and perhaps order. Their role is not merely devotional; it shows that Bacon’s universe includes finite incorporeal intellects capable of causally and cognitively affecting human minds.

Angels also matter for Bacon’s causal theory. In the Opus maius passage on species and forces, Bacon speaks of spiritual and corporeal substances producing species, of the soul multiplying force within and beyond the body, of angels moving the world by such forces, and of God producing forces in a unique creative way. This places angelic agency within the same broad causal architecture as natural agency, while preserving a distinction between created spiritual causation and divine creation.

The philosophical significance is considerable. Bacon’s system does not restrict minded agency to embodied biological organisms. There are at least four levels of mind-like or cognitive agency: God as uncreated intellect and truth; angels as created separated intellects; human rational souls as embodied intellectual souls; and animals as sensitive-cogitative souls. Bacon’s philosophy of mind is therefore hierarchical rather than human-exclusive.

There is, however, a tension. How exactly do immaterial angelic substances act upon bodies, places, and human minds? Bacon uses the language of species, force, and influence, but the precise metaphysics of spiritual-to-corporeal causation remains difficult. This is not a reason to dismiss the doctrine. It is one of the pressure points in the system: Bacon wants one universal causal language capacious enough to include light, perception, soul, angels, and God, but the more spiritual the agent becomes, the harder the model is to specify in familiar physical terms.

11. Signs, language, intention, and mental representation

Bacon’s De signis is indispensable for extracting a philosophy of mind. It gives him a theory of intentionality and communication that complements the optical and illuminative accounts.

A sign, for Bacon, can be offered either to the senses or to the intellect. Mental concepts or passions of the soul are signs, representations, and intentions of external things. Spoken words immediately refer to things rather than to mental representations.

This is a sophisticated view. On one hand, Bacon recognizes mental signs: concepts represent things. On the other hand, he denies that spoken words primarily signify concepts as internal intermediaries. Words signify things, though they do so through human acts of imposition, convention, and intention.

Bacon’s sign theory is also interpreter-centered. A sign is not simply a physical item with meaning glued onto it. It is relational: something is a sign insofar as it can lead an interpreter to conceive something. The Stanford account notes that Bacon prioritizes the interpreter in a striking way, even to the point that a thing is not a sign unless someone could conceive something by means of it.

Speaker intention is equally central. The IEP account of Bacon’s language theory says that Bacon’s grammar is intentionalist: adequacy and truth value depend on the speaker’s signifying intention, and it is not the sign that signifies on its own, but the speaker by means of language.

Bacon divides signs into natural signs and signs given by a soul. Given signs include linguistic signs, animal and human vocalizations, gestures, and other communicative acts. Some signs arise from the sensitive soul, as in animal cries or affective expressions; others arise from rational deliberation and free imposition.

This yields a powerful philosophy of mind.

First, meaning is mind-dependent but not mind-internalist. Signs require interpreters and intentions, but they signify things in the world.

Second, cognition and language are continuous with broader nature. Smoke can signify fire; animal cries can signify affective or practical states; human words can signify by rational imposition.

Third, mental concepts are representational signs. Bacon has a theory of mental content, though not in modern computational terms.

Fourth, free will enters semantics. Human naming and linguistic imposition involve rational, deliberative freedom. Later Bacon reaffirms the arbitrariness and freedom of the speaker, and he treats equivocation as important for theology and exegesis.

Fifth, language can affect the soul. Bacon’s discussions of the power of words include the idea that spoken language can affect the listener’s soul. This is not merely rhetorical ornament: it belongs to a broader theory in which signs, species, intentions, and powers move minds.

12. Experience, experiment, and certainty

Bacon’s famous commitment to experience is also a theory of mind. In his account of experimental science, he distinguishes reasoning from experience. Reasoning can lead to a conclusion, but it does not give the mind certainty or remove doubt unless the mind reaches experiential intuition of the truth. He uses examples such as knowing that fire burns: syllogism can prove, but experience gives the certainty that quiets the mind.

He then distinguishes two kinds of experience. One is through the external senses, aided by instruments and testimony. The other is internal experience, in which the intellect is aided by divine illumination. External experience is indispensable, but Bacon says it is insufficient even for full knowledge of corporeal things, and certainly insufficient for spiritual realities.

Bacon describes seven degrees of inner experience: illumination in scientific matters, virtue, the gifts of the Spirit, beatitudes, spiritual sensibilities, peace, and rapture. He also links cognition with moral condition: the mind clouded by faults is like a rusty mirror, while virtue polishes it.

This means that Bacon’s theory of certainty is not merely logical. Certainty requires experiential contact. It may require sensory experiment, mathematical ordering, inner illumination, and moral purification. The mind is not convinced simply because an argument is valid. It rests when it sees, experiences, or is illuminated.

Experimental science therefore has a psychological function: it disciplines the mind into direct acquaintance with truth. An older summary of the Opus maius captures Bacon’s distinction by saying that argument alone gives no satisfaction or certainty to the mind, which is convinced by immediate inspection or intuition; experience is external and internal, with the internal involving illumination by divine truth.

13. Error, ignorance, custom, and the pathology of mind

Bacon’s theory of mind includes a theory of cognitive failure. The first part of the Opus maius treats the four offendicula, or causes of error: weak or unworthy authority, custom, the opinion of the unskilled many, and concealment of real ignorance under the appearance of knowledge. The last is especially dangerous because it protects ignorance from correction.

These are not just methodological warnings. They are a social psychology of error. Minds go wrong because they submit to false authority, because habit makes inherited belief seem natural, because crowds confer illegitimate confidence, and because pride conceals ignorance. Bacon sees epistemic failure as moral, social, affective, and institutional.

This connects directly to his insistence on languages, mathematics, optics, experiment, and moral philosophy. The cure for error is not simply “think harder.” One must correct texts, learn languages, use mathematics, test by experience, respect good authority without servility, reform education, and purify the moral condition of the knower.

14. Will, virtue, celestial influence, and moral psychology

Bacon does not isolate cognition from will. He treats moral philosophy as the practical completion of wisdom. The IEP account notes that Bacon’s moral philosophy addresses happiness, human moral practice, and virtue, while his broader intellectual program is oriented toward the improvement of human life and the reform of theology and education.

The will appears in several places. In language, human signs require deliberate and free intellectual imposition. In moral life, virtue clarifies cognition and vice darkens it. In epistemology, the concealment of ignorance is a willful or prideful deformation of mind. In theology, the mind’s ascent toward truth is inseparable from the soul’s purification.

Bacon also accepts celestial influence, but not in a way that eliminates freedom. He thinks celestial bodies influence the body and indirectly the mind, yet he preserves freedom of the will. This adds another layer to his embodied psychology: bodily temperament and cosmic conditions can affect cognition and action, but rational and moral agency are not reducible to them.

The mature Baconian mind is therefore not a neutral information processor. It is a morally situated, socially vulnerable, bodily affected, cosmically influenced, divinely illuminated rational soul.

15. The hierarchy of minds

A comprehensive Baconian philosophy of mind should be organized hierarchically.

At the summit is God, the uncreated intellect, uncreated truth, eternal law, and Agent Intellect. God is the principal illuminator of human cognition and the ultimate ground of truth.

Below God are angels, finite created intellects. They are not always actual in the way God is, and they are not the principal source of truth, but they can cleanse, illumine, arouse, and causally influence minds and the world through spiritual power.

Human beings occupy the middle. A human is an embodied intellectual soul. Human cognition depends on bodily sense, internal sense, memory, cogitation, species, language, experiment, and divine illumination. Human beings have individuated intellects, free will, moral responsibility, and the capacity for science and virtue.

Nonhuman animals possess sensitive souls with perception, memory, affective signs, communication, and cogitative powers. They can discriminate and act intelligently in a sensory-practical way, but they lack proper rational intellect.

Plants and lower living things possess vegetative soul-powers without cognition in the relevant sense.

This hierarchy is not merely taxonomic. Each level reveals something about mind. Animal cognition shows that intentionality and discrimination precede rational intellect. Human cognition shows that intellect can be embodied. Angelic cognition shows that finite intellect need not be bodily. Divine cognition shows that truth and intelligibility are grounded in the highest intellect.

16. Extracted Baconian philosophy of mind: systematic theses

A rigorous reconstruction can be stated in twelve theses. 1. Embodied spiritualism. The human mind is not the body, but neither is it a detached Cartesian subject. It is the intellective power of a spiritual soul united to a living body and dependent in this life on sense, internal sense, and species. 2. Causal representationalism without ideationalism. Cognition requires species as causal likenesses or powers, but the object known is the thing, not the species as a private mental object. 3. Perceptual externalism. Mind is world-involving. Things act upon cognitive powers through species; signs signify things; names are imposed in relation to things; experimental knowledge depends on encounter with singulars. 4. Priority of singulars. Bacon gives strong priority to individual things and resists treating universals as the primary objects of knowledge. This supports a mind-world relation grounded in singular encounter. 5. Anti-Averroist personal intellect. Human knowledge is not the activity of one common possible intellect shared by all. Individual souls have individual intellectual acts, and knowledge is multiplied across persons. 6. Illuminationism. Human intellectual cognition requires divine illumination, especially for certainty, truth, first principles, and moral wisdom. God as Agent Intellect is the principal noetic source. 7. Angelic secondary noetics. Angels are finite spiritual intellects capable of secondary illumination and causal assistance, but they are subordinate to God’s principal influence. 8. Graded animal cognition. Nonhuman animals possess real cognitive powers: perception, memory, communicative signs, cogitation, and practical quasi-inference. Human rationality surpasses but does not abolish continuity with animal cognition. 9. Semiotic intentionality. Mental concepts, spoken words, animal cries, and natural signs all belong to a broad sign theory. Signs are relational, interpreter-dependent, and often intention-dependent. 10. Experiential certainty. Argument alone is insufficient for full certainty. The mind rests in truth through experience: external sensory-experimental experience and internal illuminative experience. 11. Moralized epistemology. Virtue and vice affect cognition. A vicious or ignorant mind is like a corroded mirror; virtue polishes the soul for clearer apprehension. 12. Social pathology of error. Cognitive failure is not merely individual miscalculation. It is socially produced by bad authority, custom, popular opinion, and prideful concealment of ignorance.

17. Major tensions and interpretive problems

The first major tension is naturalization versus illumination. Raizman-Kedar’s reading emphasizes the natural activity of species in the intellect and challenges the view that species operate only at the physical or sensory level. Hackett accepts that Bacon gives species a role in mind, but warns that material species are not sufficient for intellectual knowledge; divine illumination remains essential. The best reconstruction preserves both sides: Bacon naturalizes more of cognition than a purely Augustinian account would allow, but he does not naturalize it completely.

The second tension concerns body-soul interaction. Species theory gives Bacon a common language for physical, sensory, intellectual, and spiritual causation, but it is not always clear how the same explanatory model applies across such different domains. Corporeal species affecting a bodily organ are easier to understand than spiritual species affecting intellect or angels moving bodies.

The third tension concerns God as Agent Intellect. If God is the active intellect, what is left for human intellectual agency? Bacon’s answer is that the possible intellect, sensory powers, cogitation, reasoning, study, language, and experiment remain genuinely human. But the highest actualization of truth depends upon God. The tension is not a contradiction; it is a participatory model of cognition.

The fourth tension concerns animals and universals. Bacon grants animals impressive sensitive cognition, and some texts seem to allow them quasi-universal discrimination. But he also reserves proper rational intellect for humans. The likely resolution is that animals can classify and discriminate through cogitative powers without intellectual universal cognition in the full human sense.

The fifth tension concerns semantics. Bacon says mental concepts are signs or representations, yet also says words immediately signify things rather than concepts. This is best read as a direct-realist semantics: concepts mediate cognition psychologically, but they are not the primary semantic objects of spoken words.

The sixth tension concerns celestial influence and free will. Bacon accepts celestial influence on body and indirectly on mind, but he also preserves human freedom. The resulting psychology is neither deterministic astrology nor disembodied voluntarism.

18. What Bacon contributes to philosophy of mind

Bacon’s philosophy of mind is valuable precisely because it refuses modern separations. It does not separate perception from physics, intellect from ethics, language from ontology, human cognition from animal cognition, mind from body, experiment from illumination, or psychology from theology.

For modern philosophy of mind, Bacon offers a striking alternative to both reductive naturalism and private Cartesianism. Mind is not reducible to body, but it is deeply embodied. Representation is not arbitrary, but grounded in causal likeness. Perception is not passive reception, but active, structured, geometrical, and cognitively processed. Language is not a mere labeling mechanism, but an intentional, interpreter-dependent sign practice. Knowledge is not mere valid inference, but experiential certainty. Cognition is not morally neutral, because virtue and vice shape the soul’s receptivity to truth.

His inclusion of God and angels expands the architecture. The mind of God is the ultimate source of illumination and truth; angelic minds are finite spiritual agents that can assist or affect human cognition. For Bacon, these are not distractions from philosophy of mind. They are part of the ontology required to explain intellectual light, certainty, spiritual causation, and the hierarchy of cognitive beings.

The most faithful reconstruction is therefore this: Bacon’s philosophy of mind is an embodied, species-mediated, sign-theoretic, experience-based, morally disciplined, anti-Averroist, illuminationist theory of cognitive life within a hierarchy of minds from animals to humans to angels to God. It is not modern psychology in medieval dress. It is a different model of mind altogether: one in which knowing is a participation in the causal, semiotic, and luminous order of reality.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
